# **Theoretical and Empirical Foundations of Move Acceptance Criteria in Meta-heuristic and Hyper-heuristic Frameworks for Combinatorial Optimization**

The pursuit of optimal solutions within the realm of combinatorial optimization represents a fundamental challenge in computational mathematics and operations research. Combinatorial optimization problems (COPs) are defined by the search for an ideal object from a finite, yet often exponentially large, set of discrete items.1 Typical exemplars include the Traveling Salesman Problem (TSP), the Knapsack Problem, and the Vehicle Routing Problem (VRP), all of which reside within the complexity class of NP-hard problems.1 Given the intractability of exhaustive search in these vast landscapes, the scientific community has developed a hierarchy of solution methods ranging from exact algorithms, which guarantee optimality but suffer from prohibitive time costs, to approximation and heuristic methods that provide "good enough" solutions within reasonable timeframes.2

Within the architecture of modern meta-heuristics and selection hyper-heuristics, the move acceptance criterion serves as the decisive mechanism for navigating the search space.5 It functions as a gatekeeper, determining whether a transition from a current solution to a candidate neighbor should be permitted. This decision process is the primary tool for balancing the antagonistic yet complementary goals of exploration—the discovery of diverse, unvisited regions of the viable space—and exploitation—the refinement and optimization of solutions within a promising local basin.7 As the field progresses toward automated algorithm design and neural combinatorial optimization, understanding the nuances of move acceptance has become paramount for developing robust, scalable, and generalizable solvers.10

## **Taxonomy of Combinatorial Optimization and Search Landscapes**

The performance and suitability of a move acceptance criterion are inextricably linked to the underlying structure of the optimization problem. Combinatorial optimization problems are formally categorized into various complexity subclasses based on their approximability.1

### **Table 1: Classification of Optimization Problems by Approximability**

| Class | Definition and Characteristics | Exemplary Problems |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| NPO(I) | Equals FPTAS (Fully Polynomial-Time Approximation Scheme); can be approximated within any factor ![][image1] in time polynomial in ![][image2]. | Knapsack Problem 1 |
| NPO(II) | Equals PTAS (Polynomial-Time Approximation Scheme); can be approximated within any factor ![][image1] in time polynomial in the input size. | Makespan Scheduling 1 |
| NPO(III) | Contains problems with polynomial-time algorithms that compute solutions within a constant factor ![][image3] of the optimal cost. | Metric TSP, MAX-SAT 1 |
| NPO(IV) | Problems approximable by a ratio that is polynomial in the logarithm of the input size. | Set Cover Problem 1 |
| NPO(V) | Problems approximable by a ratio bounded by some function of the input size ![][image4]. | General TSP, Clique Problem 1 |

1

The search landscape is mathematically defined as the triple ![][image5], where ![][image6] is the space of all possible combinatorial objects (permutations, subsets, or graphs), ![][image7] is the objective function mapping each solution to a real-valued score, and ![][image8] is the neighborhood system.3 A neighborhood ![][image9] is the set of solutions reachable from ![][image10] via a single move, such as an arc exchange or a node swap.3 The topology of this landscape—whether it is smooth, rugged, or contains vast neutral plateaus—determines the likelihood of a search algorithm becoming trapped in a local minimum.3 A local minimum is a feasible solution ![][image11] such that ![][image12] for all ![][image13].3 In complex problems like the VRP, these traps are numerous and often deep, necessitating move acceptance criteria that allow "uphill" moves to escape suboptimality.6

## **Architectures of Meta-heuristics and Hyper-heuristics**

Meta-heuristics provide high-level strategies to guide the search process, often inspired by natural phenomena like evolution, social behavior, or physical cooling.9 Hyper-heuristics extend this concept by operating on a search space of heuristics rather than directly on the solution space.10 The primary motivation for hyper-heuristics is the creation of generic, "off-the-peg" solvers that can be applied to different problem instances without the need for extensive manual parameter tuning or domain-specific customization.10

### **Selection Hyper-heuristic Framework**

A selection hyper-heuristic is typically structured around a "domain barrier" which isolates the high-level strategy from the problem-specific low-level heuristics.5 The framework comprises two distinct components:

1. **Heuristic Selection:** This mechanism chooses which low-level heuristic (e.g., move, swap, or delete operators) to apply at each decision point.5 This can be done randomly, greedily, or through sophisticated reinforcement learning policies.5
2. **Move Acceptance:** Once a new candidate solution is generated by the selected heuristic, the move acceptance operator decides whether to accept or reject it.5

Move acceptance criteria are classified as deterministic—where the decision is always the same for a given set of solution qualities—or non-deterministic (stochastic)—where the decision involves a degree of randomness.6

### **Table 2: Functional Comparison of Move Acceptance Categories**

| Category | Primary Mechanism | Effect on Search Dynamics | Typical Use Case |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Elitist | Accepts only improving or equal moves (e.g., OI, IE). | Pure exploitation; rapid convergence to local optima. | Hill climbing, final refinement stages.6 |
| Non-Elitist (Deterministic) | Accepts moves based on fixed thresholds or history (e.g., GD, LAHC). | Managed exploration; allows escape from local traps without randomness. | Scale-independent optimization, VRP with time windows.19 |
| Non-Elitist (Stochastic) | Probabilistically accepts worsening moves (e.g., SA, MC). | Broad exploration; avoids premature convergence through randomness. | Highly rugged landscapes, large-scale global search.6 |
| Adaptive/Learned | Dynamically adjusts acceptance rules based on search feedback (e.g., Q-learning). | Balances exploration/exploitation in real-time. | Dynamic landscapes, neural combinatorial optimization.16 |

5

## **Deterministic and Threshold-Based Acceptance Criteria**

The limitations of simple hill-climbing, which terminates as soon as it reaches a local peak, led to the development of deterministic non-elitist criteria that manage the "uphill" budget through thresholds or history.23

### **Elitist Strategies: OI and IE**

The "Only Improving" (OI) operator is the strictest form of move acceptance, accepting a candidate ![][image14] only if ![][image15] (for minimization).6 While fast and easy to implement, it is incapable of traversing neutral plateaus. The "Improving and Equal" (IE) operator addresses this by accepting solutions of equal quality, which is crucial in heuristic search spaces often characterized by high levels of neutrality.5

### **Great Deluge Algorithm (GD)**

The Great Deluge algorithm, introduced by Dueck in 1993, utilizes a threshold known as the "water level".5 A candidate move is accepted if its objective value is better than the current water level ![][image16], regardless of its relationship to the current solution's cost.5 The threshold is typically initialized as a factor of the initial solution's cost:

![][image17]
and updated at each iteration ![][image18] using a decay rate ![][image19]:

![][image20]
In some versions, a linear update ![][image21] is used to steadily increase the selection pressure.5 GD is often preferred for its simplicity and its ability to escape local optima without the complex parameter tuning required by Simulated Annealing.19

### **Record-to-Record Travel (RRT)**

The Record-to-Record Travel algorithm is a threshold-based method that focuses on the best-known solution (the "record").14 A candidate solution ![][image14] is accepted if:

![][image22]
where ![][image23] is a threshold that typically decreases over time.14 RRT has been particularly effective in solving the Cumulative Capacitated Vehicle Routing Problem (CCVRP), where minimizing the sum of arrival times requires a search strategy that can tolerate temporary degradations in local route length to achieve a globally superior arrival sequence.14

## **Stochastic Move Acceptance and Simulated Annealing**

Simulated Annealing (SA) remains one of the most widely used move acceptance strategies, inspired by the physical process of annealing in metallurgy.15 In this framework, all improving moves are accepted. Worsening moves are accepted with a probability ![][image24] determined by the Boltzmann distribution:

![][image25]
where ![][image26] is the deterioration in cost and ![][image23] is the temperature.5

The search begins at a high temperature, making it highly likely to accept bad moves and thus promote global exploration. As the temperature cools—following a predefined schedule such as linear, exponential, or adaptive cooling—the probability of accepting worsening moves decreases, focusing the search on exploiting the current best region.6 While powerful, SA's performance is highly dependent on the initial temperature and cooling rate, and it is sensitive to the scale of the objective function, a drawback not shared by memory-based methods.15

## **Memory-Based Acceptance: LAHC and SCHC**

A significant shift in the last decade has been toward criteria that use the history of the search to set acceptance bounds, rather than relying on an external, time-dependent parameter like temperature.19

### **Late Acceptance Hill Climbing (LAHC)**

Late Acceptance Hill Climbing, introduced by Burke and Bykov in 2012, maintains a history list of length ![][image27] containing the objective values of the last ![][image27] accepted solutions.19 At iteration ![][image28], a candidate move is accepted if its cost ![][image29] is better than or equal to the cost recorded ![][image27] iterations ago (![][image30]).19

This mechanism has several profound advantages:

* **Scale Independence:** Because it compares candidate costs to previously achieved costs, it is unaffected by a linear rescaling of the objective function (e.g., multiplying all costs by a constant), which would necessitate recalibrating SA or GD.26
* **Single Parameter:** The list length ![][image27] is the only parameter, and it directly determines the processing time and thoroughness of the search.30
* **Plateau Traversal:** It is particularly effective at navigating neutral plateaus because it allows the algorithm to accept moves that do not improve upon the current solution but are still better than the historical record.30

LAHC has been successfully applied to a wide range of problems, including school timetabling, where it can improve initial solutions by 72% in the first few seconds of execution 32, and the Double Vehicle Routing Problem with Multiple Stacks (DVRPMS), where it competes effectively with branch-and-price exact methods.33

### **Step Counting Hill Climbing (SCHC)**

Step Counting Hill Climbing is a simplification of the memory mechanism used in LAHC.23 Instead of a list, it uses a single cost bound ![][image31] that remains fixed for a specific number of steps ![][image32].35 A candidate is accepted if ![][image33]. After ![][image32] steps, the bound ![][image31] is updated to the current solution's cost and the counter is reset.23 SCHC shares the scale independence of LAHC but offers a more flexible acceptance condition and has shown the strongest performance on several university examination timetabling benchmarks.35

## **Ensemble and Group Decision-Making Move Acceptance**

Hyper-heuristics have recently moved toward ensemble move acceptance, where multiple criteria are combined to make a joint decision.5 This is modeled as a group decision-making process where each move acceptance method ![][image34] returns 1 (accept) or 0 (reject).5 The ensemble decision is formulated as:

![][image35]
where ![][image36] is the strength or weight of the ![][image28]\-th method, ![][image37] is the decision function, and ![][image38] is a threshold.5

### **Table 3: Ensemble Move Acceptance Strategies**

| Strategy | Rule for Acceptance | Theoretical Foundation |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| G-AND | Accepts only if all constituent methods (e.g., IE, SA, GD) vote for acceptance. | Minority rule; emphasizes strict selection pressure.5 |
| G-OR | Accepts if at least one constituent method votes for acceptance. | Authority rule; emphasizes high exploration.5 |
| G-VOT | Accepts if a majority of constituent methods vote for acceptance. | Majority rule; provides a balanced, robust decision.5 |
| G-PVO | Accepts probabilistically based on the ratio of "yes" votes to the total number of members. | Probabilistic majority; introduces adaptive stochasticity.5 |

5

Empirical comparisons across various problem domains have shown that G-VOT is the most effective acceptance mechanism, as it mitigates the weaknesses of individual methods.5 For instance, combining the elitism of IE with the exploration of SA and the threshold-based escape of GD allows G-VOT to maintain progress on plateaus while avoiding the premature convergence often seen in standalone SA when the cooling schedule is poorly tuned.5

## **Move Acceptance in Vehicle Routing Problems (VRP)**

The Vehicle Routing Problem (VRP) and its variants represent a cornerstone of logistics optimization, where move acceptance criteria are critical for managing the complex interplay of capacity, time, and sequence constraints.4

### **The Capacitated VRP (CVRP) and Time Windows (VRPTW)**

In the CVRP, the primary challenge is the discrete nature of the capacity constraint.14 Move acceptance criteria often need to work in tandem with neighborhood operators like 2-opt or node interchange.39 For the VRPTW, the addition of customer-specific time windows ![][image39] makes the landscape even more constrained.39

Research on VRPTW has shown that integrating a Late Acceptance strategy within an Adaptive Large Neighborhood Search (ALNS) framework significantly improves the risk of escaping local optima.42 In multi-depot models, this combination has led to a 16.9% reduction in operational costs over single-depot models, and a 40.37% reduction when depot collaboration is enabled.42 The LAHC strategy is particularly valued in VRPTW because the search landscape is highly fragmented by time window constraints; a simple elitist search would frequently stall due to the lack of immediately improving moves that also satisfy all temporal constraints.19

### **VRP with Backhauls and Loading Constraints**

The VRP with Backhauls and Time Windows (VRPBTW) requires vehicles to deliver goods to "linehaul" customers before picking up from "backhaul" customers.40 A study on the TA\_VRPBTW meta-heuristic utilized Threshold Accepting combined with modified Nearest Neighbor procedures.40 The findings demonstrated that this combination could reduce the average fleet size from 18.54 to 15.18 vehicles and improve routing time by over 28%.40 The effectiveness of Threshold Accepting here is attributed to its deterministic ability to accept "bad" moves within a certain range, which allows the algorithm to reorganize the sequence of linehaul/backhaul visits to find more efficient global tours.40

Similarly, for the Double VRP with Multiple Stacks (DVRPMSHD), which involves complex loading plans, LAHC has been shown to be highly competitive with Simulated Annealing and branch-and-price algorithms, finding high-quality solutions for these NP-hard variants by effectively managing the trade-off between tour length and stack feasibility.33

### **Table 4: Impact of Move Acceptance on VRP Performance**

| Variant | Criterion Applied | Performance Metric Improvement | Source |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| VRPTW | ALNS \+ LAHC | 40.37% cost reduction (collaborative) | 42 |
| VRPBTW | Threshold Accepting | Fleet size reduced by 18.1% | 40 |
| CCVRP | Record-to-Record Travel | Competitive CPU and cost vs. Memetic | 14 |
| DVRPMSHD | LAHC | Competitive with Branch-and-Price | 33 |

## **Modern Trends: Learning and Adaptive Move Acceptance (2024–2025)**

The landscape of meta-heuristics is currently undergoing a transformation driven by Reinforcement Learning (RL) and automated machine learning (AutoML).15 The focus has shifted from manually designing acceptance rules to learning them from the search context.12

### **Reinforcement Learning and Q-Learning Controllers**

The AHPQA framework represents a 2025 state-of-the-art approach that uses a Q-learning-driven high-level controller to select among categorized low-level heuristics (constructive, improvement, perturbation) while employing a Simulated Annealing acceptance criterion to govern the exploration-exploitation balance.16 This two-layered architecture allows the algorithm to adapt to the ruggedness of the current search region, switching to more aggressive perturbation heuristics and higher acceptance probabilities when it detects stagnation.16

In smart contract optimization, RL-based hyper-heuristic controllers are being used to optimize transaction rules in real-time.21 These controllers can adapt execution parameters (like slippage thresholds in decentralized exchanges) based on on-chain volatility and gas price fluctuations, achieving up to a 45.6% increase in transaction success rates.21

### **Neural Combinatorial Optimization and Preference Optimization**

A major 2025 development is the emergence of Preference Optimization for neural combinatorial models.11 Standard RL approaches for VRP often suffer from diminishing reward signals in vast action spaces. Preference Optimization transforms quantitative rewards into qualitative preference signals via statistical comparison modeling, emphasizing the superiority among sampled solutions.11 By integrating local search into the fine-tuning phase, this method achieves significantly better convergence on TSP, CVRP, and Flexible Flow Shop Problems compared to traditional RL algorithms.11

Furthermore, the LSFlow approach introduced in 2025 utilizes spherical flow matching to learn a stochastic policy in a compact continuous latent space, delegating feasibility to a combinatorial solver that maps these latent samples to valid structured actions.46 This hybridizes the strength of deep learning's representation capabilities with the rigorous constraint-handling of traditional optimization solvers.

### **Information Bottleneck and Representation Learning**

The application of Information Bottleneck-Enhanced (IBE) Reinforcement Learning to COPs addresses the issues of high-dimensional state spaces and inefficient exploration.47 By integrating the information bottleneck principle with attention-based RL architectures, researchers have improved both the representation learning and the robustness of RL agents in operations research tasks.47 These data-driven heuristics are increasingly capable of generalizing across diverse problem instances, offering a flexible alternative to handcrafted algorithms.47

## **Exploration vs. Exploitation: Theoretical Trade-offs**

The core of move acceptance research lies in the theoretical trade-off between exploration and exploitation.7

### **Table 5: Theoretical Trade-off Mechanisms**

| Parameter/Mechanism | Influence on Exploration | Influence on Exploitation |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Temperature (SA) | High ![][image23] promotes accepting large cost increases. | Low ![][image23] restricts moves to local improvements. |
| History List Length (LAHC) | Large ![][image27] increases the "memory," allowing long-term uphill search. | Small ![][image27] leads to faster, more localized convergence. |
| Mutation Rate (GA) | High rate introduces random diversity into the population. | Low rate preserves the structure of elite solutions. |
| Acceptance Bound (SCHC) | Long cycles (![][image32]) hold the bound fixed, allowing more uphill steps. | Short cycles update the bound quickly, increasing greediness. |

6

Excessive exploration leads to high computational costs and slow convergence as the algorithm wanders through unpromising regions.8 Conversely, excessive exploitation causes premature convergence to a local optimum, as the search space is not sufficiently sampled to identify the global peak.8 Modern meta-heuristics address this by using adaptive strategies that shift the balance based on progress—for example, reducing the temperature in SA or using "reheating" when stagnation is detected.8

The concept of "elitism" in evolutionary computation provides another perspective: by preserving the best solutions from one generation to the next, algorithms ensure that high-performing genetic material is not lost, while diverse selection methods (like tournament selection) allow less fit individuals to survive, thus promoting exploration of new niches in the fitness landscape.49

## **Practical Implementations and Future Outlook**

The practical application of move acceptance criteria is now supported by advanced software frameworks and automated solver generators.20

### **Heuristic Generation and LLMs**

An emerging trend (2024-2025) is the use of Large Language Models (LLMs) to automatically design heuristic operators within frameworks like Large Neighborhood Search (LNS).51 These models, like VRPAgent, generate problem-specific "destroy" and "repair" heuristics that have been shown to outperform handcrafted methods on standard CVRPLib benchmarks.51 Grammatical Evolution is also being used as an online solver builder, taking various acceptance criteria and neighborhood structures as inputs to evolve templates for perturbation heuristics.12

### **Green and Energy-Efficient Optimization**

With the increasing computational demands of RL-based optimization, "Green Automated Reinforcement Learning" has become a priority.52 Research in 2025 has highlighted the energy consumption disparity between different COPs; for instance, solving a Sequential Ordering Problem (SOP) can consume significantly more energy than an Asymmetric Traveling Salesman Problem (ATSP), even if the energy usage per minute is similar.52 This necessitates the development of energy-efficient RL models that track real-time power usage and optimize the trade-off between solution quality and environmental impact.52

### **Automated Solvers vs. Heuristic Frameworks**

Comparative studies between commercial solvers like Gurobi or OR-tools and specialized heuristic frameworks (e.g., RoutBench) show that for many VRP variants, specialized heuristics are far more effective.38 In instances with complex constraints (e.g., CVRPTW with duration limits), heuristic frameworks achieve success rates up to four times higher than standard solvers because they allow for more flexible constraint-handling and specialized move acceptance strategies that can navigate feasible regions more efficiently.38

The move acceptance criterion, once a simple choice between accepting or rejecting a move, has evolved into a sophisticated, often learned, decision-making agent. Whether through the robust, parameter-lite memory of LAHC, the collective intelligence of G-VOT ensembles, or the high-dimensional policies of reinforcement learning, the goal remains the same: to efficiently traverse the vast landscapes of combinatorial optimization to reach the peak of optimality. As we move into an era of sustainable and automated algorithm design, the integration of context-aware, adaptive move acceptance will be the defining characteristic of the next generation of global optimization tools.
